section.para: The contracting of intellectual property in the digital economy is undergoing a seismic shift. Traditional models based on the sale of goods or the licensing of finished assets are increasingly challenged by legal frameworks that re-characterize the creation of IP as an act of service. This shift, driven by the economic realities of platform labor and stringent, retroactive worker classification laws (particularly in jurisdictions like California), exposes systemic vulnerabilities in how companies acquire IP rights. This report analyzes the spectrum of IP acquisition—from full purchase to licensing—and explores the implications for user-platform relations, peer-to-peer networks, and the emerging landscape of digital assets and IP securities. The central finding is that the distinction between a "good" and a "service" is the critical legal fault line, transforming contractual disputes into fundamental questions of labor rights and fiduciary duty.
